question_title: Will the interest in “princess charlotte celebrated her eleventh birthday with a new photograph” change between 2026-05-04 and 2026-05-16 according to Google Trends?
summary: Princess Charlotte's 11th birthday photo was released on May 2, 2026, driving the Google Trends value for this very specific 11-word query to 100. The question compares May 4 (2 days after peak) vs May 16 (14 days after peak) values. May 16 will almost certainly be 0 — news event search queries reach zero within 5-10 days. The key uncertainty is whether May 4 still shows a value >3 on the 0-100 scale. For typical news events, day-2 values are 15-30% of peak, strongly favoring Decreases. However, this is an extremely specific 11-word sentence query with very low absolute search volume, creating a meaningful chance that Google Trends rounds the May 4 value to 0 due to low volume thresholds — which would make both dates ~0 and resolve as "Doesn't change." Increases is near-impossible (requires unexpected renewed interest in this exact topic 14 days later). I estimate ~70% Decreases, ~28% Doesn't change, ~2% Increases.

factors.description: News event peaked on May 2 (Princess Charlotte birthday photo); May 4 is only 2 days after peak. Research shows day-2 values typically retain 15-30% of peak for news events, likely well above the ±3 threshold.
May 16 is 14 days after the peak. Research shows virtually all news event queries reach zero within 5-10 days. The May 16 value is almost certainly 0.

The query is an 11-word news headline sentence, which is extremely specific. Very few people search full sentences, meaning absolute volume is very low. Google Trends may round the May 4 value to 0 due to low volume threshold, causing both dates to be ~0.

Royal family birthday events have broad mainstream appeal with coverage across all major outlets, suggesting sustained search interest for at least 2-3 days — this argues May 4 still has measurable value above 3.

No plausible mechanism for renewed interest in this specific query by May 16. No follow-up events expected. Increases would require an unexpected new event generating searches for this exact long phrase.

reasoning:
**Forecast Summary:**

| Outcome | Probability |
|---------|------------|
| **Decreases** | **70%** |
| Doesn't change | 28% |
| Increases | 2% |

**Key reasoning:**
- Princess Charlotte's birthday photo was released May 2 (peak = 100). By May 4 (2 days later), residual search interest likely remains above the ±3 threshold. By May 16 (14 days later), interest is almost certainly at 0 → **Decreases** is the most likely outcome.
- The main source of uncertainty: this is an extremely specific 11-word sentence query with potentially very low absolute search volume. If Google Trends rounds the May 4 value to 0 (below reporting threshold), then both dates would be ~0 → **Doesn't change**.
- **Increases** is near-impossible — there's no mechanism for renewed interest in this exact phrase 14 days after the birthday.

resolution_criteria: This question resolves “Doesn't change” if the value on the timeline at [https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=princess%20charlotte%20celebrated%20her%20eleventh%20birthday%20with%20a%20new%20photograph&date=2026-04-16%202026-05-16](https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=princess%20charlotte%20celebrated%20her%20eleventh%20birthday%20with%20a%20new%20photograph&date=2026-04-16%202026-05-16) for 2026-05-16 is within 3 of the value at 2026-05-04. It resolves “Increases” if the value is more than 3 greater, and resolves “Decreases” if the value is more than 3 lower.
fine_print:
A script will be used to determine the resolution of this question. It will access the data on Google Trends using [SerpApi](https://serpapi.com/), and compare the height of the timeline for the two aforementioned dates. The specific python query will be structured as follows:
```
from serpapi import GoogleSearch

search = GoogleSearch(params={
  "api_key": API_KEY,
  "engine": "google_trends",
  "data_type": "TIMESERIES",
  "geo": "US",
  "tz": 0,
  "q": "princess charlotte celebrated her eleventh birthday with a new photograph",
  "date": "2026-04-16 2026-05-16",
})
search.get_dict()
```
Note that there may be differences between the results returned by the API and the data appearing on the page. This seems to be due to the 'tz' parameter not having the intended effect in-browser. In this case, the API results will be considered authoritative.
Note that the precision of the timeline will be 1 day, so this will compare the overall interest for the whole day as determined by Google Trends.
Dates are determined in UTC.
If the data is no longer available, or the script fails, this question will be annulled or manually resolved by a moderator.

factors.description: News event peaked on May 2 (Princess Charlotte birthday); May 4 is only 2 days after peak, so residual interest is likely. Google Trends research shows day-2 values typically 15-30% of peak for news events.
May 16 is 14 days after the peak - research shows virtually all news event queries reach zero within 5-10 days. The May 16 value is almost certainly 0.

The query is an 11-word news headline sentence, which is extremely specific. Very few people would search this exact phrase, meaning absolute volume is very low. Google Trends may round the May 4 value to 0 due to low volume threshold.
Royal family birthday events have broad mainstream appeal, suggesting sustained search interest for at least 2-3 days - this argues May 4 still has measurable value above 3.
No plausible mechanism for renewed interest in this specific query by May 16. No follow-up events expected for this exact topic. Increases essentially requires an unexpected new event.

input.anchor: For Google Trends questions where a spike occurred near the start date: historical pattern shows ~60-70% Decreases, ~25-30% Doesn't change, ~5% Increases based on similar Metaculus questions with news event spikes.
input.assessment:
The question hinges on whether the Google Trends value for this very specific 11-word news headline query still shows measurable interest (>3 on the 0-100 scale) on May 4, two days after the peak. May 16 will almost certainly be at 0. 

The key tension: on one hand, the news event (Princess Charlotte's 11th birthday photo) generated enough interest for a peak of 100, and news event search interest typically retains 15-30% of peak at day 2. On the other hand, the query is so specific (a full sentence) that absolute search volume may be extremely low, causing Google Trends to round the day-2 value to 0 due to reporting thresholds. If May 4 ≤ 3 and May 16 ≈ 0, the resolution is 'Doesn't change.' If May 4 > 3 and May 16 ≈ 0, the resolution is 'Decreases.'

My estimate weights ~70% on Decreases (May 4 likely retains some interest above the threshold) and ~28% on Doesn't change (both values effectively zero). Increases is near-impossible at ~2%.
input.calibration_notes: The anchor of ~65% Decreases comes from similar Google Trends Metaculus questions where a news spike occurs near the start date. My estimate of 70% Decreases is close to this anchor. The main adjustment is the very specific nature of the query, which creates more uncertainty about whether day-2 values are above or below the reporting threshold. My estimate feels reasonable - not overconfident on Decreases despite the strong theoretical case, because the low-volume threshold effect is genuinely uncertain.
input.key_uncertainties: The primary uncertainty is whether Google Trends shows a non-zero value for this extremely specific 11-word query on May 4 (day 2). If May 4 > 3, it's Decreases; if May 4 ≤ 3, it's Doesn't change. I cannot directly check the current Google Trends value, so this remains the key unknown.
input.tool_audit: Research sub-agent provided useful decay pattern analysis for news event Google Trends queries. Web search confirmed the Princess Charlotte birthday event on May 2 with photo release. Google Trends URL fetch failed (JS rendering issue, expected for Google Trends). Monte Carlo simulations were useful for quantifying scenarios. SerpApi access not available to check actual current values.
input.update_triggers: 1. Accessing the actual Google Trends data for May 4 would resolve the key uncertainty. 2. A significant royal family news event around May 16 could increase interest (unlikely). 3. Evidence about how Google Trends handles very long unquoted queries would refine the model.
input.process_reflection: The main limitation is not being able to check the actual Google Trends data for today (May 4). The Google Trends page requires JavaScript rendering which failed. Having SerpApi access or a Google Trends API tool would be extremely valuable for these questions. The research sub-agent provided good theoretical background on decay patterns but couldn't access real-time Google Trends data either. The Monte Carlo approach was helpful but the uncertainty about Google Trends' exact handling of long queries without quotes is the biggest gap.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Checks Performed

### Resolution Criteria — PASS
Resolution criteria are fully provided and correctly engaged. The agent properly identifies: the ±3 threshold, the May 4 vs May 16 comparison dates, the April 16-May 16 resolution window, SerpAPI as authoritative source, daily precision, and UTC dates.

### Hallucinated Evidence — PASS
All core factor claims are grounded in the research trace:
- **Factor 1** (day-2 values 15-30% of peak): Confirmed by the worldview research entry which states "Day 2: 15-20%" for birthday/photo events and the general research entry saying "Day 2: 15-30%."
- **Factor 2** (queries reach zero within 5-10 days): Confirmed by research: "Most specific one-off event queries reach effectively zero within 5-10 days."
- **Factor 3** (low-volume rounding to 0): Grounded in research citing Jun et al. (2018) about needing ~1,000+ daily searches for stable data.
- **Factor 4** (royal family broad mainstream appeal): Confirmed by web search results showing coverage from Today.com, Hello Magazine, E! Online, GB News, ITV News, etc.
- **Factor 5** (no mechanism for renewed interest): Reasonable analytical judgment, no factual claim to verify.

### Wrong-Direction Factor — WARN
**Factor 5** has a logit sign that contradicts its description. The description states: "No plausible mechanism for renewed interest in this specific query by May 16. No follow-up events expected for this exact topic. Increases essentially requires an unexpected new event." This clearly argues AGAINST Increases and thus FOR Decreases. However, the logit is -0.5, which pushes AWAY from the named outcome ("Decreases"). The sign should be positive to support Decreases. Impact: small magnitude (-0.5) and the other factors dominate, so correcting this wouldn't meaningfully change the probability.

### Double-Counting — MINOR (not rising to warn)
Factors 1 and 2 both draw on the same decay pattern evidence. Factor 1 addresses day-2 residual interest (May 4) and Factor 2 addresses day-14 decay (May 16). They target different temporal aspects of the same underlying phenomenon but share the same evidence base (news event decay literature). The overlap slightly inflates the apparent evidence for Decreases but doesn't change the directional forecast.

### Contradictory Assessment — PASS
The assessment correctly identifies the key tension (whether May 4 value is above 3 given the long query), weighs both scenarios, and reaches a conclusion consistent with the factor analysis.

### Probability Assessment — PASS
My independent estimate: ~65-75% Decreases, ~23-33% Doesn't change, ~2% Increases. The agent's 70/28/2 falls squarely within my range. The core uncertainty — whether a full 11-word news headline sentence query retains measurable Google Trends interest two days after peak — is genuine and well-articulated. The agent's own simulations ranged from 55% to 100% Decreases depending on assumptions about low-volume thresholds, making ~70% a reasonable central estimate. The past "tsa funding" retrodict (where the agent predicted 47% Doesn't change but it resolved Decreases) slightly suggests the agent may be over-weighting the Doesn't change scenario, but that was a different query type.

### Anchor Divergence — PASS
Anchor is 60-70% Decreases. Final probability is 70% Decreases — at the top of the anchor range but not divergent.

### Weak Counterargument — PASS
The counterargument is genuinely strong: it constructs a coherent alternative centered on the 11-word query being essentially unsearchable, explicitly challenges the "onlyfarms" analogy, and argues P(Doesn't change) should be 40-50%. This is a real alternative story, not a token gesture.

### Overconfident Self-Assessment — PASS
Confidence of 0.40 is appropriately low given: inability to access actual Google Trends API data, genuine uncertainty about how Google Trends handles extremely long unquoted queries, and the key May 4 value being unknowable from available information.

### Tool Audit — MINOR NOTE
The agent's tool audit claims "Google Trends URL fetch failed (JS rendering issue, expected for Google Trends)." Ground truth confirms 1 fetch_url call with 0 errors, but the content returned was empty/unhelpful (only browser compatibility message). The characterization as "failed" is slightly inaccurate (it didn't error, it just returned no useful data), but this is immaterial.

### Pre-Publication Event — N/A
Forward-looking question comparing May 4 (published date) to May 16.

### Cross-Question Consistency — PASS
The same agent forecasted 92% Decreases for "british royal family" (q43373) with the same May 4 vs May 16 dates. The much lower 70% here is justified by the legitimate concern that this 11-word sentence query has dramatically lower search volume than a common 3-word term.

## Summary
One wrong-direction factor (Factor 5, logit -0.5 should be positive) that doesn't materially affect the forecast. The probability estimate of 70% Decreases / 28% Doesn't change / 2% Increases is reasonable and within my independent assessment range. The core uncertainty about long-query Google Trends behavior is genuine and well-handled.
